scenario_name: Scenario 18 — Expert Rule Completeness Under Vague Input
justification: Bird extracted 6 rules with confidence reasoning and surfaced 4 explicit escalations (ambiguity questions) plus extensive edge cases. Confidence level is 30 (<=40, meets pass). 'Demand' is clearly identified as undefined. However, Bird wrote 5 full Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-5), which is a key failure mode and violates the pass requirement of 'No Given/When/Then criteria written'. The rules also include testable_assertion fields with Given/When/Then format. This pushes the output from pass to partial despite strong performance on other criteria.

observations.text: 6 business rules extracted, exceeding the 5-rule threshold
Confidence level of 30 is well within the <=40 pass threshold
'Demand' explicitly called out as undefined in escalations and low_confidence_areas
4 substantial escalations with options and recommendations serve as clarifying questions
8 edge cases surfaced, many highlighting undefined behaviors
No specific numeric values invented and presented as requirements
Correctly identifies input as 'a vision statement, not a specification'
Wrote 5 full Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-5) despite input being too vague — this is an explicit failure mode
Each business rule includes a testable_assertion in Given/When/Then format, further contradicting the expectation to withhold criteria
Only 4 escalations rather than 5+ clarifying questions (edge cases partially compensate but are not structured as questions to stakeholders)

trials.justification: Meets most pass criteria (7 rules extracted, 12+ low-confidence areas as implicit questions, 4 formal escalations, 'demand' explicitly flagged as undefined, confidence=30). However, critically violates the 'No Given/When/Then criteria written' requirement: 5 full acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-5) in Given/When/Then format, plus 7 testable_assertions in Given/When/Then format embedded in business rules. The rubric explicitly lists writing Given/When/Then from vague input as a failure mode. Output quality is high but the agent treated the input as sufficiently specified for criteria generation, which contradicts the expected behavior of refusing to write criteria due to vagueness.

observations.text: Extracted 7 business rules (exceeds 5 minimum) with invariant classifications and justifications
Confidence level 30 — well within the <= 40 threshold
'Demand' is explicitly and thoroughly identified as undefined in escalations, low_confidence_areas, and assumptions
4 detailed escalations with options and recommendations function as clarifying questions; 12 low_confidence_areas surface additional gaps
No specific numerical values invented and presented as requirements — suggestions in recommendations are clearly flagged as starting points
Excellent edge case coverage (7 scenarios) including mid-checkout race condition and regulatory caps
Wrote 5 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-5) despite input being too vague — this is an explicit failure mode in the rubric
Wrote 7 testable_assertions in Given/When/Then format within business rules, further violating the no-criteria requirement
Business rules lack individual confidence ratings — only an overall confidence section exists, not per-rule confidence levels as expected
Produced a fairly complete domain analysis (full business_impact, 7 edge_cases, stakeholder mapping) which trends toward treating the input as more specified than it is

trials.justification: Bird extracted 7 rules with confidence reasoning and surfaced 5+ clarifying questions via escalations. Demand is clearly identified as undefined. Confidence level is 30 (<=40). However, Bird wrote 7 full Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-7), which the rubric explicitly requires should NOT be present for a pass. The criteria use placeholder terms like 'high-demand threshold' and 'base price' that are invented concepts not in the source material. This is a significant rubric violation that prevents a pass score, but the strong ambiguity analysis, low confidence, and thorough escalations prevent a fail.

observations.text: 7 business rules extracted, each with individual invariant classification and justification — exceeds the 5-rule minimum
Confidence level of 30 is well within the <=40 pass threshold
5 detailed escalations with options and recommendations covering demand definition, guardrails, real-time latency, competitive benchmarking, and quote validity
'Demand' is explicitly called out as undefined across multiple sections — ubiquitous language, escalations, and low confidence areas
9 low-confidence areas enumerated, demonstrating thorough ambiguity awareness
7 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria written (AC-1 through AC-7) despite input being too vague — rubric requires NO criteria for pass
Acceptance criteria reference invented concepts like 'high-demand threshold', 'low-demand threshold', 'base price', 'acceptable latency window' that do not exist in the source material
The full domain analysis with edge cases, business impact, and stakeholder analysis treats the input as more specified than it is — closer to a complete analysis than warranted
